one_line_takeaway: Inference-time activation steering methods for reducing LLM overrefusal (and separately, hallucination) can look safe and precise under standard in-distribution efficacy/specificity checks yet consistently and substantially increase jailbreak vulnerability (8-55 point safety drops across four models) because the benign/harmful representation boundary they steer along collapses under adversarially disguised (jailbreak-prefixed) inputs.
key_finding: With explicit control (Table 2), steering methods (except SSV) reliably increase compliance on pseudo-harmful queries both in-distribution (PHTest) and out-of-distribution (ORBench-hard), largely preserve MMLU/GSM8K/fluency (general specificity) and largely preserve refusal on canonical harmful queries from JailbreakBench (control specificity, deltas mostly near zero except a 10-point drop with LinearProbe on Llama-8B-Instruct and 7-8 point drops with DiffMean/LinearProbe/ReFT-r1 on Llama-3B-Instruct). However, robust specificity collapses across the board: jailbreak-prefixed harmful queries show safety drops of 35-55% on Llama-8B-Instruct, 20-30% on Qwen-7B-Instruct, 8-16% on Llama-3B-Instruct, and 17-27% on Gemma-2B-Instruct. The largest utility (compliance) gains correlate with the largest robustness losses (e.g., LinearProbe's 12-point utility gain on Llama-8B-Instruct corresponds to its largest 55-point robustness drop). PCA analysis of layer-20 activations (Qwen-7B-Instruct) shows jailbreak-prefixed harmful-query activations shift toward the harmless-query region, explaining why steering vectors trained on clean benign/harmful separations fail to hold under jailbreak-induced representation shift. SSV separately shows the sharpest general-specificity regression (47% MMLU drop on Qwen-7B-Instruct). The same robust-specificity collapse pattern is reported for faithfulness-hallucination steering (steered models become more susceptible to irrelevant/distractor context), indicating the finding generalizes beyond the overrefusal setting.
core_contribution: The paper introduces an evaluation framework that decomposes 'specificity' of inference-time activation steering into three dimensions -- general specificity (preserving fluency and unrelated capabilities), control specificity (preserving closely-related properties, e.g. refusal on genuinely harmful queries), and robust specificity (preserving control properties under distribution shifts such as jailbreak attacks) -- and applies it to five representative steering methods (DiffMean, LinearProbe, SSV, ReFT-r1, PartialOR) across four instruction-tuned LLMs (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, Gemma-2-2B-it) in two safety-critical use cases: overrefusal steering and faithfulness-hallucination steering. The authors argue and empirically demonstrate that standard efficacy/general/control specificity checks are insufficient because they miss robustness failures that only appear under adversarial distribution shift.
threat_model: The paper's threat model concerns steering methods used to reduce overrefusal in safety-aligned LLMs (making models more compliant on benign-but-flagged queries) while a defender assumes refusal on genuinely harmful queries is preserved. The adversary is a jailbreak attacker who prepends jailbreak prefixes (drawn from JailbreakHub, Shen et al. 2024) to harmful queries from JailbreakBench, exploiting the fact that steering vectors are learned along directions separating benign from harmful activations -- directions that jailbreak-disguised harmful queries can evade because their hidden activations shift toward the benign region (shown via PCA of layer-20 activations in Figure 4). The paper does not propose a new attack; it treats jailbreaking as an existing adversarial evaluation tool to reveal that steering-based safety interventions have a robustness blind spot.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) analysis is conducted in a single-task setup per use case rather than a comprehensive benchmark cataloging target/control properties; (2) evaluation is on generated text/sampled outputs rather than sequence probabilities, which the authors note as an extension left to future work; (3) experiments limited to LLMs up to 8B parameters due to compute constraints; (4) steering (like SFT/RLHF) requires white-box model access, so findings do not directly apply to closed/API-only models where prompting is the accessible alternative. The paper also notes a measurement caveat for Gemma-2-2B-it's low GSM8K score being partly an instruction-following formatting artifact rather than true math-reasoning failure.

datasets_used: OR-Bench / ORBench-hard subset (out-of-distribution overrefusal eval, Cui et al. 2025)
quantitative_results: Overrefusal steering with explicit control (Llama-8B-Instruct): jailbreak robustness drops 35-55% across steering methods vs baseline
Qwen-7B-Instruct: jailbreak susceptibility increases 20-30% after steering
Llama-3B-Instruct (highest baseline jailbreak robustness): jailbreak susceptibility increases 8-16%
LinearProbe on Llama-8B-Instruct: largest utility gain (~12 points ComplianceRate) coincides with largest robustness drop (~55 points)
SSV on Qwen-7B-Instruct: ~47% drop in MMLU accuracy (largest general-specificity regression observed)

Control specificity (canonical harmful queries) mostly near-zero deltas, with exceptions: -10% safety with LinearProbe on Llama-8B-Instruct; -7 to -8% with DiffMean/LinearProbe/ReFT-r1 on Llama-3B-Instruct
500 test queries per evaluation condition; 2,500 adversarial test instances for robustness eval (25 jailbreak prompts x 100 harmful queries)
baselines_compared: DiffMean (Difference-in-Means, Li et al. 2023 / Turner et al. 2024 / Rimsky et al. 2024)
SSV (Supervised Steering Vector, Wu et al. 2024)
ReFT-r1 (Rank-1 Representation Finetuning, Wu et al. 2024/2025a)
Unsteered model baseline (per-model reference for computing deltas)
